segments.s717-g9: This clause is a verbless predicate: the pronoun تو (you) plus the comparative داناتری (more knowing) states a quality of the addressee. The preposition به introduces the domains 'ایران و نیران' that the knowledge applies to; Persian often omits an explicit copula, so the sense is 'You are knowledgeable about/in ...'.
segments.s717-g10: همان here works as a demonstrative/emphatic particle introducing a second remark about the same person; بر زبان is a prepositional phrase ('on the tongue' = 'in speech'), with بر used in the spatial/schematic sense, and تواناتری is the comparative adjective 'more able' predicated of the same subject (the pronoun is omitted but understood). The clause is parallel to the previous verbless predicate and contrasts domains of expertise (knowledge vs. speech).
segments.s717-g11: در گنج بگشاد is a simple past narrative clause: the locative در + گنج (treasure/treasury) is the object/place, and بگشاد is an archaic past form of گشودن ('he opened'). The coordinating و links the following quantity phrase چندان گهر ('so many jewels'); چندان modifies گهر to indicate a large amount.
segments.s717-g12: بیاورد is a past narrative verb 'he brought' (third-person singular). It takes a coordinated noun phrase as its object: 'شمشیر و زرین کمر' = 'swords and a golden girdle', where زرین is an adjective modifying کمر. Word order is verb after object(s) in earlier/formal Persian narrative sequences is common; here the verb follows the linked nouns.
segments.s717-g13: که introduces a result or explanatory clause ('so that' / 'such that'). خراد (an archaic form of خرد) functions as a nominative noun 'wisdom' or 'reason', and برزین is a personal name; بران here is a locative/relational particle 'upon/at that' or 'thereon', and خیره ماند is a compound predication: خیره 'astounded/staring' + ماند 'remained' (past), i.e. 'remained astonished'.
segments.s717-g14: همی is an adverb of continuative aspect ('still/kept on'); در نهان is a locative phrase 'in secret'; نام یزدان is the object 'the name of God', and بخواند is a past narrative verb here meaning 'he invoked/called (the name)' (an older literary form of خواندن). The clause therefore means 'he continued to call/invoke the name of God in private.'
segments.s717-g15: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. With the adverbial با هدیه‌ها ('with gifts') the main clause follows: راه چین بر گرفت is an idiomatic narrative construction where راه چین ('the road to China') is the path taken and بر گرفت (compound verb 'bar-gereft' split here) = 'he took/up/took up' or 'set out toward'. Note چین is the place-name 'China' here, not the common noun 'wrinkle'.
segments.s717-g16: This clause gives a second, contrasted movement: به جیحون ('to/at the Jeyhun' — the river Amu Darya) sets the location, یکی راه دیگر ('one [i.e. a certain] other road') expresses that he chose a different route, and گرفت is the past verb 'took'. The sequence means that when he reached the Jeyhun he took another way.
segments.s717-g49: The correlative negation 'نه ... نه ...' connects two instrumental phrases: 'نه با اینش مهر' = 'not by/through this one's love' and 'نه با آنش کین' = 'nor by that one's hatred'. 'با' is the instrumental 'by/through'; 'اینش' and 'آنش' are demonstrative + enclitic -ش ('this/that of him/her') referring to the possessor; word order places the instrument before the noun.
segments.s717-g50: 'نداند' is the classical negative present (or narrative present) of 'دانستن' meaning 'does not know'; the indefinite noun 'کس' ('anyone') follows the verb (classical word order). 'این' is the demonstrative 'this' (referring back to the matter just asserted). 'جز' here means 'except', and 'جهان‌آفرین' is the exception ('the World‑Creator').
segments.s717-g51: The clause begins with 'که' introducing an explanatory clause ('for/since/that'). 'یک سر' literally 'one head' = 'a single origin' placed before the topicalized phrase 'همه خاک را' ('the whole earth' marked by object marker 'را'). 'زاده‌ایم' is perfect first‑person plural 'we have borne/gotten birth' (classical use can be active or reflexive—here it conveys originating/being born from one source).
segments.s717-g52: 'به' marks the indirect object ('to/for the wretched body'); 'بیچاره تن' = 'the wretched/poor body' (adjective before noun). 'مرگ را' is the direct object marked by 'را' ('death' as the thing given), and 'داده‌ایم' is perfect 'we have given/caused' — here best read as 'we have appointed/inflicted death upon the wretched body.'
segments.s717-g53: 'نخست' = 'first/at first'. 'اندر آیم' literally 'I come into' (here narrative: 'I came'); 'ز جم' = 'from Jam' (the preposition 'ز' = 'from' in poetic register). 'برین' is a demonstrative/adjectival element 'this/high/foremost' formed of 'بر' + 'ین' (the final 'ین' echoes the demonstrative 'این') and attaches forward to the following noun, so the whole phrase means 'to this foremost ...'.
segments.s717-g54: 'جهاندار' is a title 'sovereign' or 'world‑ruler' followed by the proper name 'طهمورث' (Tahmūraš). 'بافرین' is an epithet/adjective 'praiseworthy' (lit. 'with praise') that qualifies the name; the three words form a nominal phrase 'the sovereign Tahmūraš the Praiseworthy.'
segments.s717-g55: 'چنین هم' = 'thus/so as well'; 'برو' is the imperative 'go (on)' addressing the listener or the narrative voice. 'تاسر' = 'up to/until'. 'کی قباد' is the royal name 'Kay Qobad' (here 'کی' functions as the dynastic/title element 'Kay', not the interrogative 'who'), so the whole segment means 'proceed up to Kay Qobad.'
segments.s717-g56: 'همان نامداران' = 'those same renowned ones' (همان = 'the same/that very'); 'که' is the relative pronoun 'who/that'. 'داریم' is present 'we have/hold' and with 'یاد' ('memory, remembrance') forms the idiom 'که داریم یاد' = 'whom we remember' or 'whose memory we keep.' Word order is the classical SOV/relative clause placement.
segments.s717-g57: برین here is best read as بر این (compound written together): بر ‘upon/on’ + این ‘this’, so the phrase means “upon this” or “fix this on”. هم (also) modifies نشان (mark/token). تا به + name introduces the indirect object/target (‘to Esfandiyar’). Word order is topical: the object-marker phrase (بر این نشان) comes before the clause showing the recipient (تا به اسفندیار).
segments.s717-g58: چو is the comparative/simile ‘like/as’. It links the following names to the prior action: ‘like Kay-Khosrow and Rostam’. و is the normal coordinative 'and'. نامدار is an epithet modifying both names: ‘famed/renowned’. The sequence is a short simile comparing Esfandiyar’s treatment to that of those two heroes.
segments.s717-g59: ز گیتی functions as a poetic locative ‘in/of the world’. یکی دخمه شان بود is a verbless/nominal clause finished by بود ‘was’: ‘there was one tomb that was theirs’. شان is the pronominal possessive ‘their’. بهر here introduces a causal or purposive sense linking to what follows (‘for that reason’ / ‘for it’).
segments.s717-g60: چشیدند is the past plural ‘they tasted/experienced’. بر جای is an idiom ‘in place of / instead of’ (lit. ‘upon the place’), followed by تریاک زهر where تریاک ‘opium’ is contrasted with زهر ‘poison’: the clause says they tasted poison in place of opium (i.e., they were given poison instead of an anodyne). Word order places the verb first (poetic) and the contrastive noun phrase after.
segments.s717-g61: کنون is the temporal adverb ‘now’. شاه ایران is the nominal subject ‘the king of Iran’. به‌تن خویش is a reflexive/locative phrase ‘in his own body / in person’ (خویش = ‘own’), and the following ش is the possessive clitic attached to indicate ‘his’. تست here functions as the copula/predicate (‘is’ / ‘is so’ in older poetic usage). The overall line states the king’s presence in person.
segments.s717-g62: همه ‘all’ is the topic; شاد و غمگین lists two classes ‘the joyful and the sorrowful’. به کم بیش is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘to a lesser or greater extent / more or less’ (کم ‘less’, بیش ‘more’) and qualifies possession or relation to the king. The final تست repeats the copular sense (old/poetic), so the clause says everyone — both glad and sad — is, more or less, his.
segments.s717-g63: به هنگام شاهان is a temporal phrase ‘at the time of kings’ or ‘in the days of kings’. با آفرین is an instrumental or accompanying phrase ‘with praise / with acclamation’: با + noun means ‘with’ and آفرین here carries the sense of public praise or acclaim rather than the verb ‘to create’. The segment sets the scene and the manner (‘with praise’) in which what follows was true.
segments.s717-g64: پدر مادرش بود is a standard nominative + predicate construction: ‘his father and mother were…’ with ش marking the possessive ‘his’. خاقان چین is the predicate nominal: ‘the Khagan (supreme ruler) of China’ — here چین denotes the place/name China, so the parents are given that lofty title. بود is the past copula ‘was/were’. Word order places the title after the verb for emphasis (poetic inversion).
segments.s717-g33: نخست is an adverb meaning 'first/at first' placed at the clause start for emphasis. آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (praise/exclamation) + کرد (past of کردن) = 'offered praise' or 'praised'. بر introduces the beneficiary of the praise (بر + NP = 'toward/for'), and کردگار is the addressee 'the Creator/God' — so the whole phrase means 'first praised the Creator.'
segments.s717-g34: This is a string of epithets describing کردگار. توانا is an adjectival epithet 'powerful/able' placed before the following nominal phrase. داننده is an active-participle noun 'knower' that takes روزگار as its object (in classical Persian the Ezāfe is often omitted in poetry), so داننده روزگار = 'knower of fate/time.' Together: 'the powerful knower of fate.'
segments.s717-g35: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' referring back to کردگار. The list چرخ و مکان و زمان are coordinated direct objects ('sky/heaven, place, and time') followed by the past-tense verb آفرید at the clause end — the typical Persian SOV (objects ... verb) order in poetry.
segments.s717-g36: This parallel noun phrase continues the description of what he created: توانایی ('ability') and ناتوان ('the incapable/powerless'), joined by و, with the verb آفرید repeated (or understood) to conclude the clause. Note the poetic pairing of opposites — ability vs. inability — formed by the negative prefix نا- on توان.
segments.s717-g37: Nominal phrase: همان 'that same' modifies چرخ 'the wheel/sky', followed by the present-participle گردنده 'turning/revolving' as a postpositive modifier and the prepositional phrase بی ستون 'without a pillar' (بی = without + ستون = pillar). This forms a descriptive headline that the following clause comments on.
segments.s717-g38: چرا نه is a rhetorical 'why not' (why would it not ...). به فرمان او = 'by/under his command' (به + فرمان = 'by command', او = 'his'). در نه چون is an elliptical/classical expression here meaning roughly 'to be within/in it' or 'to be subject to it' (در = in/within; نه/چون are older particles used for emphasis/structure); overall the clause asks why that revolving sky would not be under God's command. (Word order is rhetorical and compressed, typical of epic Persian.)
segments.s717-g39: بدان is the imperative 'know' formed from the prefix ب + the verb دان ('to know') — here a direct address: 'know (this)'. آفرین functions as 'آفریننده' (creator) in this poetic compression. کو is an archaic/poetic relative particle equivalent to 'who/that' linking to the following clause, and جهان آفرید ('created the world') places the past verb آفرید at the end as usual.
segments.s717-g40: This is a coordinated two-part predicate describing the Creator's actions: بلند آسمان گسترید = 'he raised/highened the lofty sky' (بلند = lofty/high, آسمان = heaven), and و زمین گسترید = 'and he spread/extended the earth' (گسترید is past tense 'spread/extended'). The verb گسترید governs both preceding noun phrases; the order places modifiers before their nouns, common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s717-g25: بدو گفت is literal 'to him he said' — the indirect-object pronoun بدو precedes the verb (archaic word order) and گفت is the past 3rd-sg. The subordinate clause هرگه که فرمان دهی uses هرگه که = 'whenever/when', followed by فرمان (object 'command') and دهی, the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive of دادن meaning 'you give/issue' (here 'you issue [an order]').
segments.s717-g26: به + گفتن: به here introduces the action 'for/to speaking' (the verbal noun گفتن). زبان is the instrument/agent of that speaking ('the tongue' = speech). بر گشاید is the verb 'opens' where بر functions as a separable verbal prefix (poetically written apart) and گشاید is 3rd‑sg. 'open'. رهی is a poetic form (cf. ره) meaning 'a way/path' (poetic/archaic noun).
segments.s717-g27: بدو گفت خاقان = 'the Khaghan said to him' with بدو (to him) before گفت (he said) and خاقان as the subject; word order is somewhat inverted. به شیرین زبان is an ablative/measure phrase 'in sweet tongue/with sweet words' — به here marks the manner ('in', 'with') and شیرین زبان = 'sweet‑tongued / with sweet speech.'
segments.s717-g28: دل مردم = 'the heart(s) of the people' (مردم in genitive relation). پیر گردد جوان is a predicative clause with گردد (from گشتن) as 'becomes' (3rd‑sg. present/subjunctive/formal) and پیر juxtaposed to جوان for contrast: 'old becomes young' → 'though old, (their) hearts become young.' The adjective جوان is the resulting state.
segments.s717-g29: بگو is the imperative 'say'. آن سخن‌ها 'those words' is the direct object. که introduces a relative clause 'that are (of) use'; سود is 'benefit/use', and اندروست is an archaic locutive/copular expression meaning roughly 'is/are therein' or 'therein is' (i.e. 'are of use').
segments.s717-g30: سخن گفت = 'the spoken word(s)' (گفت here functions adjectivally/predicatively as 'said/spoken'). مغز‌ست is 'is the marrow/essence' (مغز + ست, copula), so 'spoken words are the marrow'. و connects the clauses; ناگفته is a past-participle used substantively 'the unsaid/untold', and پوست is the predicate 'skin/surface' — a contrast between inner value (مغز) and outer form (پوست).
segments.s717-g31: چو = 'when'. خراد برزین is a personal name/title (خراد + برزین) functioning as the subject. شنید is simple past 3rd‑sg 'he heard', and آن سخن is the object 'that speech'. Word order follows classical Persian narrative pattern (temporal marker + subject + verb + object).
segments.s717-g32: به‌یاد آمدش is an idiom 'it came to his memory' — به‌یاد 'to memory' + آمد + clitic -ش (to him) attached to آمد, literally 'to memory came him'. کینهای کهن = 'ancient/old hatreds' (کینهای = plural 'hatreds/grudges', کهن = 'ancient/old'), object of the memory verb.
segments.s717-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی آمد is a simple past clause ('information/news came/arrived'); به شاه بزرگ marks the indirect object 'to the great king' and appears after the verb because of poetic word order (the king as recipient is placed at the end of the clause).
segments.s717-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. از بیشه بیرون is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the thicket'; خرامید is a past tense verb 'strode/strutted' (poetic past) and گرگ, the subject, follows the verb as is common in epic/poetic word order.
segments.s717-g3: سپاهی is the noun 'an army/force' placed before the verb for emphasis; بیاورد is a perfective/past or jussive form of آوردن 'bring/raise/send' (here: 'raised/sent'); بهرام گرد is the personal name placed after the verb (name + epithet). Poetic word order places the agent at the clause end.
segments.s717-g4: که introduces the purpose/relative clause 'that/so that'. از آسمان روشنایی is 'light/brightness from the sky'; ببرد is a subjunctive/jussive third-person form of بردن used to express intended action or result ('would/should take/carry away').
segments.s717-g5: به marks the indirect object 'to'; خراد برزین is the addressee (a name composed of an epithet 'خراد' = 'wise' + name 'برزین'); چنین is 'thus/so'; گفت is the past tense verb 'said'; شاه, the subject 'the king', appears at the clause end in poetic word order.
segments.s717-g6: که introduces the reported content. بگزین is an imperative/subjunctive form of گزیدن 'choose' (an instruction). بر + ین together form the archaic demonstrative 'برین' = 'this'; کار is 'work/task/deed'; the second بر here indicates duration or 'for' and is followed by چار ماه 'four months' (چار = 'four', ماه = 'months').
segments.s717-g7: یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone'; سوی means 'toward/to'; خاقان is the title 'Khaghan' (the ruler to whom the envoy will go); بی‌مایه is an adjectival epithet 'without worth/worthless'; پوی is an epithet 'swift/runner' describing the envoy. Word order lists the envoy and his epithets before the verb implied later.
segments.s717-g8: سخن is the object 'words/speech'. هرچ دانی is a free relative 'whatever you know' (هرچ = 'whatever', دانی = 2nd-person present 'you know'). که introduces the clause 'that'; باید is the modal 'must/ought to'; بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form of گفتن here meaning 'say/tell'. The sequence is a command: tell whatever you know that ought to be told.
segments.s717-g17: چو+past-verb is a temporal clause: چو here = “when.” نزدیک (an adjective/adverb) precedes the noun it modifies (درگاه) and means “near/close to.” درگاهِ خاقان is a genitive phrase “the khaghan’s court.” رسید is the simple past (3rd sg.) ‘arrived/reached’; the subject is understood from context.
segments.s717-g18: نگه کرد is a light‑verbal compound: نگه (a noun “gaze/stop/pausing”) + کرد (past ‘did’) producing the verb ‘paused/looked.’ و simply coordinates the next clause. گوینده‌ای is indefinite (‑ای) “a speaker/ spokesman,” and برگزید is the past (3rd sg.) verb ‘chose/selected.’
segments.s717-g19: بدان here functions as a directional/dative phrase ‘to him’ (i.e. to the chosen speaker) and combines be‑+pronoun in classical spelling; تا introduces purpose/result ‘so that/in order that.’ بگوید is a present/subjunctive form used after تا to express purpose (‘may say’ or ‘should say’). که introduces the content clause. از نزد شاه = ‘from the presence of the king,’ with نزد marking ‘in the presence of.’
segments.s717-g20: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause ‘a messenger came.’ بدین is the classical form of به + این ‘to this,’ marking direction toward this palace (here split in the word list); بارگاه = ‘royal court/palace.’ آمد is past (3rd sg.).
segments.s717-g21: چو + بشنید = ‘when he heard.’ The verb بشنید is past (3rd sg.). خاقان is the subject (title ‘khaghan’). بیاراست is past (3rd sg.) from آرَستن ‘to adorn/array/prepare,’ here reflexive or causative ‘he made himself/ the court ready.’ گاه is a noun ‘occasion/time’ tied to the preparation.
segments.s717-g22: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past ‘he commanded/said.’ تا introduces the purpose/result clause (‘so that’). برگشادند is past (3rd pl.) of برگشودن ‘to open’ and here indicates that doors/ways were opened; the verb is plural (they opened / were opened). راه ‘way/path’ is the object or affected noun; word order is typical poetic inversion.
segments.s717-g23: فرستاده آمد = ‘the messenger came’ (past 3rd sg.). به تنگی is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in narrowness,’ i.e. ‘in embarrassment/constraint’ or ‘under difficulty’; به marks the manner. فراز here links to ‘زبان کرد’ that follows, roughly ‘aloft/high’ or ‘with raised voice’ in this poetic usage.
segments.s717-g24: زبان کرد is another light‑verbal construction: زبان (speech/tongue) + کرد (‘did’) = ‘he spoke’ or ‘he put forth speech.’ کوتاه modifies the manner ‘briefly.’ و coordinates the next action. بردش is here an idiomatic verb form meaning ‘he offered/took (to) someone’ in the phrase بردنِ نماز (classical usage for offering salutations); the provided lemma senses do not match this usage. نماز in court register can mean ‘salutation’ or ‘prayer/greeting’ and here means the formal greeting/salutation he paid.
segments.s717-g137: کسی را — "کسی" is an indefinite object ("someone") marked by the post‑positional object marker "را". "فرستاد" is simple past (3rd pers. sing.) meaning "sent"; the subject is omitted (understood from context). The conjunction "و" links the second action: "او را بخواند" where "او را" is the direct object and "بخواند" is a past/epic perfect form meaning "called/summoned" (here causative/active: he caused him to come). Word order places object before the verb, as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s717-g138: بران — an adverbial/deictic "on that / thereon" that attaches to the action that follows. "نامور جایگاهش" is a noun phrase: "نامور" (renowned) modifies "جایگاه" (place/seat) with the 3rd‑person possessive suffix "‑ش" ("his/its"). "نشاند" is an archaic past (causative) meaning "(he) seated/caused to sit" (cf. modern "نشاندن"); it describes the completed action of placing someone on the seat.
segments.s717-g139: The clause reads as a series of past actions giving money: "درم داد و دینار داد" — "gave a dirham and gave a dinar." The pronouns before the verbs are compressed in epic style: "او را" is the direct object "him," and "داد" is simple past "gave." The initial form "مر" is an archaic/pronominal element (see note) placed for emphasis or dative reference; the two verbs are coordinated by "و."
segments.s717-g140: "همان پوشش" = "that same cloak/garment" ("همان" = the same/that; "پوشش" = cloak/covering). "و خورد بسیار داد" — in this poetic line "خورد" functions as a nominalized sense referring to food/edibles ("to eat/food") and is governed by the verb "داد" (past "gave"); "بسیار" qualifies it as "much/plenty." The structure is two coordinated direct gifts: clothing and abundant food were given.
segments.s717-g141: "چو" = "when" (temporal conjunction). "بر خوان نشستی" = "you sat at/onto the (banquet) table" — "بر" + "خوان" (banquet/table) with "نشستی" as the 2nd‑person past "you sat" (epic past). "ورا خواندی" = "you called/summoned him" — "ورا" is the object pronoun "to him/that one," and "خواندی" is past tense of "خواندن" used here in the sense "to call/summon." (Note: the lemma shown for "نشستی" in the entry is not the verbal root for "نشستن"; here it functions as "you sat.")
segments.s717-g142: "بر نامدارانش بنشاندی" — "بر" (among/upon) introduces the position; "نامدارانش" = "his/her/their notables/renowned ones" (the plural/collective of "نامدار" with possessive "‑ش"). "بنشاندی" is 2nd‑person past causative/perfect of "نشاندن" meaning "you caused (him) to sit/you seated (him)." The clause states that you placed him among the notables.
segments.s717-g143: "پر‑اندیشه" is a compounded adjective meaning "full of thought/pondering" or "deeply thoughtful." The following words form a descriptive noun phrase: "بد مرد" and "بسیاردان." In epic Persian adjectives and qualifiers can stack; "مرد" is the head noun "man." "بسیاردان" means "very learned/knowledgeable." (The short word "بد" here is part of the older adjectival cluster and should be read as an archaic modifier within the descriptive string.)
segments.s717-g144: This segment lists complementary qualities: "شکیبا دل" literally "patient of heart" (adjective + noun used adjectivally), then coordinated adjectives "زیرک و کاردان" = "shrewd/clever and capable/skillful." The coordinating "و" links the positive attributes; the whole phrase completes the portrait of the man described.
segments.s717-g177: چنین داد پاسخ که is a reporting phrase: چِنـین (thus) + داد (gave, past simple) + پاسخ (answer) with که introducing the content of the reply. این را has the object marker را marking این as the definite/direct object. بدار is an imperative (= “keep/hold [it]”) used here in classical/poetic Persian to command keeping or retaining the object; it is the content of the reported speech introduced by که.
segments.s717-g178: بخواهم is first-person present/subjunctive form used here with an optative/future sense (“I will ask / I shall request”). هر آنگه که forms a temporal subordinate clause meaning “whenever / each time that” (هر = each/every, آنگه = then/that time, که = subordinating 'that/when'). آید به کار is a verbal idiom: آید (come/happen) + به کار (be of use); together it means “becomes useful / is needed.”
segments.s717-g129: 'تو' is the subject 'you'. 'مردی دبیری' is a predicative nominal phrase meaning 'a man of dābīrī' (man of office/letters), with the possessive/relational noun following the head noun (noun + genitive-like postmodifier). 'یکی چاره‌ساز' is an appositive: 'یکی' = 'a certain' or 'one' introduces the noun 'چاره‌ساز' (problem‑solver) as a free apposition describing the same person.
segments.s717-g130: 'وزین نیز' is an adverbial/parenthetical modifier meaning 'even/also weighty' (qualifying the secret). The construction 'بر باد مگشای راز' literally 'do not open/reveal the secret to the wind' uses the prohibitive form 'مگشای' (negative imperative/prohibitive from گشودن, here 'do not open/disclose') governing the object 'راز', while 'بر باد' = 'to the wind/into the wind' expresses the idea of letting it be blown away (i.e. divulged).
segments.s717-g131: 'چو' functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'خراد برزین' is a compound personal name (Kharād Barzīn) serving as subject, followed by the past tense verb 'شنید' ('he heard') and the object 'این سخن' ('these/this words'). Word order is the normal SOV for Persian narration (subject + past verb + object).
segments.s717-g132: This line uses correlative negation 'نه ... نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. The verb 'دید' ('saw') governs both noun phrases 'سر' and 'بن' (the two things not seen). 'پیمان او را' ('his covenant' + object marker 'را') specifies which head and foundation are meant; the object phrase is placed between the two negated nouns for poetic/elliptical effect.
segments.s717-g133: 'یکی' is the indefinite introducer 'a certain'. The noun phrase 'ترک بد پیر' stacks adjectives before/after the noun: 'ترک' (a Turk) + 'بد' (bad/ill) + 'پیر' (old man). 'نامش قلون' is a short verbless clause 'his name (was) Qalun' where '-ش' is the enclitic possessive 'his' and the name follows as predicate (typical of Persian verbless equational constructions).
segments.s717-g134: 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who/whom'. 'ترکان' is the subject 'the Turks' and 'ورا' (archaic variant of 'او را') is the object 'him'. 'داشتندی' is an archaic past plural ('they had/kept/held') with the old plural ending '-ندی'. 'زبون' here is a predicative adjective meaning 'abject/humiliated', so the clause means 'the Turks kept him humiliated' or 'they held him in abasement'.
segments.s717-g135: 'همه پوستین بود' is a terse descriptive clause 'everything/all was a fur coat'—poetically meaning 'he wore only a fur coat'. 'پوشیدنش' is the nominalized form of 'پوشیدن' with the clitic '-ش' ('his/it'), literally 'his/its wearing', used here to summarize the act of wearing that single garment; the line compresses state and action for effect.
segments.s717-g136: The preposition 'ز' ('from/of' in poetic diction) introduces the items he ate: 'ز کشک و ز ارزن' = 'kashk and millet'. 'بدی خوردنش' is a compact clause: 'بدی' ('badness/ill fare') modifies 'خوردنش' (the nominalized 'his eating/consuming'), so together they mean he fared poorly eating those foods (i.e. subsisted badly on kashk and millet).
segments.s717-g89: همی‌گفت is a habitual/past narrative expression (همی + گفت) meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying'; the verb comes first here, followed by the subject بهرام. بدگوهر is an adjective (lit. 'bad‑gem') meaning 'ill‑natured / of bad character', and است is the copula 'is' completing the predication in reported speech.
segments.s717-g90: از introduces origin/agency here ('from' or 'coming from') linking آهرمن (Ahriman/the devil) to بد‌کنش ('bad‑deed, misdeed'). بدتر است is a comparative clause: بدتر 'worse' with the copula است 'is', so the whole phrase says that a misdeed that comes from Ahriman is still worse.
segments.s717-g91: فروشد is a narrative past form meaning 'he sold' (or 'he caused to be sold'); جهاندیدگان (literally 'those who have seen the world') means 'worldly‑wise people' or veterans; را is the definite/object marker marking جهاندیدگان as the object; به + چیز 'for/for a thing' expresses the price or exchange.
segments.s717-g92: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('which/that'); آن چیز 'that thing' is the referent; گفتن is the verbal noun 'saying/mentioning' (here 'to mention'); نیرزد is a negative present/formal subjunctive meaning 'is not worth'; پشیز is a small unit/figurative 'a trifle' — the clause says 'that thing is not worth a pashiz (a mere trifle) to mention.'
segments.s717-g93: ورا is a dative/clitic meaning 'to him' or 'to that one'; هرمز is the subject (Ahura Mazda/Hormaz); تاجور ('tâjūr') means 'merchant' or 'trader' as the direct object; برکشید is a past‑tense verb 'lifted/raised' used metaphorically to mean 'exalted' or 'promoted'.
segments.s717-g94: به ارجش = 'to/for his honour' (ارج 'honour/value' + ش enclitic 'his'); ز خورشید برتر uses ز (poetic 'from/of/than') in a comparative sense 'than the sun', خورشید 'the sun', and برتر 'higher/superior'; کشید here is another past form of کشیدن meaning 'drew/raised' (metaphorically 'exalted'), so the line says his honour was raised higher than the sun.
segments.s717-g95: ندانست is the past negative of دانستن meaning 'did not know' (here part of a negative existential sense); کس is 'anyone/someone' but with the negation it yields 'no one'; در جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world'; نام اوی = 'his name' (نام 'name/reputation' + اوی 'his').
segments.s717-g96: ز گیتی بر آمد is a past clause 'from the world arose' (ز = 'from/of', گیتی 'the world', بر + آمد 'came up/arose' — بر here gives the sense 'up/forth'); همه کام اوی 'all his desires' (همه 'all', کام 'desire/wish', اوی 'his'), meaning that every wish of his was granted or came from the world.
segments.s717-g97: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). با تو is a prepositional phrase marking the beneficiary/target ('with you'); word order is typical Persian preposition + pronoun. بسیار خوبی is a noun phrase literally 'much goodness/kindness' where خوبی (goodness, kindness) is the deverbal noun and بسیار quantifies it. کند is 3rd-person singular present subjunctive/indicative used here in the protasis (protasis of a conditional) and literally 'does' — together the segment means 'if he is very kind to you / if he does much kindness to you.'
segments.s717-g98: به فرجام is a temporal/goal prepositional phrase 'at/in the end' (به + فرجام). پیمان تو places the possessed noun 'pact/covenant' with the clitic-like possessor تو following the noun (classical Persian order: noun + possessor). بشکند is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive/jussive form with the prefix b- (بشکند) derived from شکستن 'to break' and here expresses the result clause ('he will break / will violate').
segments.s717-g99: چنان هم که is a connective phrase ('such/just so that / just as'); که introduces the subordinate clause. با شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase indicating the opponent/partner in the contest ('with / against the Shah of Iran'). شکست here is a past/result verbal form meaning 'he was defeated / lost' (classical Persian past 3rd-person sense) rather than the nominal 'breakage'; the clause therefore reads 'just as he lost against the Shah of Iran.'
segments.s717-g100: This clause omits an explicit copula (typical in Persian) and lists two coordinated nominal compounds formed with the suffix پرست '‑worshipper'. نه ... و نه is the negative correlative 'neither ... nor'. خسرو پرست is 'Khosrow‑worshipper' (i.e. a court-lover/king‑worshipper) and یزدان‌پرست is 'God‑worshipper' (pious person). The phrase as a whole says he was neither one nor the other.
segments.s717-g101: گر is an archaic conditional particle equivalent to 'if'. او را uses the object marker را after the pronoun to mark 'him' as the direct object of فرستی. فرستی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of فرستادن 'to send' ('you send' / 'if you send'). به نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'to/near the king' where به + نزدیک gives the idea 'into the king's presence / close to the king.'
segments.s717-g102: سر شاه ایران is a genitival sequence 'the head of the Shah of Iran' (noun + possessor). بر here is the preposition/prefix 'upon / up onto' and combines with the following verb. آری in this classical line functions as an archaic subjunctive/optative form meaning 'will bring / will raise' (from آوردن) — in older Persian poetry such forms differ from modern colloquial forms; if the reader prefers a literal reading, treat آری as the verb 'bring/raise'. به ماه is the goal 'to the moon'. The whole segment thus says 'he will bring/raise the Shah's head to the moon.'
segments.s717-g103: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time after / thereafter' (از + آن + پس). همه چین و ایران is the compound nominal 'all China and Iran' (همه quantifier + two place‑names joined by و). تو راست places the pronoun 'you' before راست ('right/true/upright'), a predicate adjective implying rightful possession or that you will be established in right; the terse order (noun phrases with predicate omitted) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s717-g104: نشستن‌گه is a compound noun 'place of sitting / seat' (نشستن + گاه). آنجا 'there' is the locative adverb pointing to that place. کنی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/do' used here to mean 'you shall make/establish.' کت is a short noun (in the manuscript here; context suggests a high seat — note: in some copies this may read تخت 'throne'), and هواست combines هوا 'desire/aspiration' with the copula ست 'is' to mean 'it is desire/it belongs to desire / it is by will.' Grammatically the segment describes making that place your seat and states the throne/seat is characterized by desire/will.
segments.s717-g41: A series of adjectives/nouns (توانا و دانا و دارنده) is presented before the copula اوست; this is a nominative/predicative string describing the subject "he." ست is the archaic/poetic form of the copula (equivalent to است) and follows the personal pronoun او. Word order (adjectives → pronoun + copula) is typical in literary Persian.
segments.s717-g42: سپهر و زمین را is a coordinated definite object (the heavens and the earth) marked by the object marker را for emphasis/definiteness, followed by نگارنده اوست: a nominal predicate meaning 'he is the painter/adorner.' نگارنده is an agentive/nominal form and the copula اوست identifies him as that agent; the clause therefore reads 'he is the one who adorned/painted the heavens and the earth.'
segments.s717-g43: به چرخ اندرون is a prepositional/locative phrase ('into/within the sky') placed before the verb for poetic order; آفتاب is the direct object and آفرید is the past-tense, third-person singular verb 'created.' آفرید is the classical past of آفریندن used here as simple past 'he created.'
segments.s717-g44: A coordinated list of objects (شب و روز و آرام و خواب) precedes the single verb آفرید; this is a common Persian order where multiple direct objects are listed and the verb applies to all. آرام here functions as a noun meaning 'calm/peace/rest', pairing with خواب 'sleep.' آفرید again is past tense 3rd sg 'he created.'
segments.s717-g45: توانایی is the subject noun 'ability/power' and اوراست is a compact/poetic expression meaning 'it is his' (the form compresses او را است → اوراست). The second clause ما بنده‌ایم uses بنده as a predicate noun 'we are servants' with the present plural copula -ایم; بنده here functions as a humble/self-deprecating role (typical devotional phrasing).
segments.s717-g46: همه modifies راستیهاش ('all his truths' — راستیها plural + possessive suffix -ش 'his'), forming the object/theme. گوینده‌ایم is 'we are tellers/speakers' with the present plural copula -ایم; the verb/noun asserts that the speakers proclaim all of his truths. The possessive enclitic attaches directly to the plural noun (راستیها + ش).
segments.s717-g47: یکی را uses را to mark the person receiving the action (definite/emphatic object/topic). دهد is the third-person singular present (or gnomic/present-tense narrative) 'gives.' تاج و تخت بلند are the things given; بلند is an adjective ('high/lofty') that qualifies تخت (and by coordination effectively the pair). The verb precedes the gift in poetic order but functions as a simple transitive 'he gives.'
segments.s717-g48: Another parallel clause: یکی را (another person) is the object and کند is the 3rd-person singular 'makes/does' (from کردن). بنده و مستمند are predicate nouns/adjectives: 'a servant and needy/poor.' The structure means 'he makes one [to be] a servant and needy.'
segments.s717-g145: وزان is a literary contraction (و + از آن) meaning 'and/from that' or 'for that reason'; روی here combines with وزان to convey 'for that reason' or 'from that side'. با is the preposition 'with'. کدخدای سرای is a noun phrase 'the headman/steward of the house' (کدخدا = headman, سرای = household/house), forming a prepositional phrase 'with the household steward'. The clause is verbless and links forward to the next segment which supplies the action.
segments.s717-g146: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking source or agent. خاتون چینی is a noun + adjective: 'the Chinese lady' (خاتون = lady, چینی = Chinese). همی‌گفت is an old/habitual past form (همی- prefix + گفت) meaning 'would say' or 'kept saying' — here it denotes the repeated/ongoing act of consulting. رای is the object 'counsel/opinion'; together همی‌گفت رای means 'would ask/seek counsel'.
segments.s717-g147: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same'. پیش خاقان means 'in the presence of the khagan' (پیش used as 'before, in front of / in the presence of'). به‌روز و به شب are parallel adverbials 'by day and by night' (note the prefixed به used adverbially). The verb is omitted (a common poetic device), so this verbless clause denotes habitual presence at court.
segments.s717-g148: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رفتی is 2nd-person past 'you went/you left' (here addressing 'you' in direct speech). همی‌داشتی is a past-habitual form (همی‑ + داشتی) meaning 'you used to keep' or 'you would keep'. بسته لب is a collocation 'with lips closed' (بسته = closed, placed before the noun), an idiom for keeping silent. The clause contrasts being at court with keeping silent when departing.
segments.s717-g149: چنین 'thus' is a discourse adverb introducing what follows. گفت is the past tense verb 'he said' (here used in the narrative). با + مهتر آن مرد پیر is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the elder chief' (مهتر = distinguished elder/chief; آن مرد پیر = that old man). In poetry word order can be inverted; here the agent and addressee frame the reported speech.
segments.s717-g150: که introduces a subordinate clause (here giving the reason/explanation). چون functions as 'since' (causal). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. سرافراز is a predicative adjective 'honored/lofty', and مردی + دبیر flesh out the predicate: مردی = 'a man' (or 'manly person') and دبیر = 'secretary/scholar' used as a predicate noun. Word order is Subject (تو) + predicate adjectives/nouns.
segments.s717-g151: اگر is the conditional 'if'. در پزشکیت = 'in your medicine/your medical skill' (در marks the domain; the possessive -ت marks 'your'). بهره بدی literally 'give benefit/share' — بهره = 'benefit/share' and بدی here functions as the verb 'you give' in the conditional (an archaic/poetic form; orthography may compress بدهی → بدی). The clause means 'if you render any benefit in your medical skill'.
segments.s717-g152: وگر is the alternative conditional 'or if' / 'but if'. نامت = 'your name/reputation' (نام + possessive -ت). از دور = 'from afar' (از = from; دور = distant). شهره here means 'fame/renown' (often used predicatively: 'to become famed'). بدی again is the verb form parallel to the previous clause (archaic/poetic 2nd-person form 'you give/become'), so the line reads 'or if your name becomes famed from afar.'
segments.s717-g161: بیامد is simple past, 3rd singular ('he came'). به + خراد is a prepositional phrase 'to (the) Khurad' where خراد is an epithet meaning 'wise' used like a title; برزین is the personal name that follows the epithet. بگفت is a narrative past ('and said' / 'he said'), joining the two actions (came and said). Word order is literary: verb (بیامد) + prepositional phrase + name + reporting verb.
segments.s717-g162: The initial که introduces reported content ('that'). این راز is the clause's subject/object phrase 'this secret'. باید is an impersonal/modal 'must/it is necessary' and is followed by a subordinate clause introduced again with که. داری is 2nd-person present 'you keep/have' (here 'keep'); نهفت is a past-participial/adjectival form 'hidden/concealed' functioning as complement, so باید که داری نهفت = 'you must keep (it) hidden.'
segments.s717-g163: برو is the 2nd-singular imperative 'go'. پیش او = 'in his presence / before him' (پیش as 'in the presence of'). نام خود را is the definite object 'your name' with خود emphasizing 'your own' and را marking it as the direct object. مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not say/tell' (classical negative of گفتن). The clause orders movement plus the prohibition about revealing the name.
segments.s717-g164: پزشکی is the noun 'medicine' used with the imperative کن ('do! / act!') to mean 'act as a physician' or 'practice medicine' (imperative). از here has a role-marking sense 'as / in the guise of' (i.e. 'as a ...'), خویشتن is reflexive 'yourself', and تازه‌روی is 'a newcomer/novice'. Together the phrase instructs to perform the physician's role while presenting oneself as a novice.
segments.s717-g165: به نزدیک + خاتون is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the lady'. شد is past/inchoative 'came / arrived' (here 'came near'). آن چاره‌گر is the noun phrase 'that healer' (آن = 'that', چاره‌گر = 'one who finds remedies'). The sequence places the motion verb before the subject in a literary order: 'came near the lady that healer.'
segments.s717-g166: تبه functions adverbially/expressively here ('woefully' / 'ill-fatedly'); دید is the simple past 'he saw'. بیمار is the adjective/noun 'sick' applied to جگر 'liver' (used poetically as 'heart' / seat of feeling). او را with را marks possession/definiteness ('her' / 'of her') — classical word order is inverted, placing بیمار (sick) before the possessive phrase and the noun, yielding 'he saw, sadly, that her heart was sick.'
segments.s717-g167: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'she/he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'that / so that', and آوردند (آورند) is a 3rd-plural jussive/optative here 'they bring' or in narrative 'they should bring / they brought'. آب نار is a genitive-like phrase 'water of fire' = 'hot water'. The whole clause = 'She ordered that they bring hot water.'
segments.s717-g168: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' (here 'that same / immediately'), تره means 'green herb(s)', and جویبار 'stream' works attributively to indicate the herb from the brook (a compound sense: 'stream-herb' or 'herb of the stream'). آورند repeats the 3rd-plural 'they brought'. Together: 'They brought that same herb from the stream.'
segments.s717-g73: بران گاه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'thereupon/at that time' (بران = 'thereupon', گاه = 'time'). جایی is an adverb/object meaning 'somewhere'. بپرداختش is a past-tense verb form with the enclitic object -ش ('him'); here, in archaic/epic usage, it means 'set/placed (him)' rather than the literal senses given in the lemma list.
segments.s717-g74: به نزدیکی خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to/near his nearness' = 'near himself', where به = 'towards/at', نزدیکی = 'nearness', and خوی + ش is the noun with the possessive enclitic -ش ('his'). بنشاختش is past 3rd-person with the object clitic -ش and is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'made him sit' or 'seated him'.
segments.s717-g75: به فرمان او is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by his command' (به used as 'by' or 'by means of'). هدیه‌ها is the direct object 'the gifts'. پیش برد here is a compound verbal idea 'brought forward/presented' (پیش = 'forward/in the presence of', برد is the past of بردن 'to carry/bring' with the object implied).
segments.s717-g76: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one'. به گنج‌ور او is a dative phrase 'to his treasurer' (به = 'to', گنج‌ور = 'treasurer', او = 'his/he'). برشمرد is past 3rd-person 'he enumerated/numbered (them)'.
segments.s717-g77: بدو is the prepositional/enclitic phrase 'to him' (به + او contracted). گفت is the past of 'to say' (here 'said'), though the provided lemma senses are for 'speech' rather than the verb form, so use of the verb sense is implied. خاقان is the vocative/subject 'the Khaghan'. که introduces the content clause 'that', and بی‌خواسته is an adverb meaning 'unwillingly/unintentionally' modifying the following clause.
segments.s717-g78: مبادی is plural of 'mabda' meaning 'beginnings/origins/sources' (here: foundations or sources of status). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. اندر = 'in/within'. جهان = 'the world'. کاسته is a past participial/adjectival-past form meaning 'diminished/lessened' and predicated of 'مبادی تو'.
segments.s717-g79: گر is the conditional 'if'. از من is 'from me'. پذیرفت is here the lexical root 'accept' (classical ordering places the verbal root before the auxiliary). خواهی تو is a future/volitional auxiliary construction 'you will/would' with تو ('you') as subject; together the phrase means 'if you will accept a thing from me'. چیز is the indefinite 'a thing/anything'.
segments.s717-g80: بگو is the imperative 'say' (addressing the addressee). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that' or 'in order that'. پذیرم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive 'I may accept' or 'I will accept'. من is the explicit subject pronoun 'I' (emphatic). آن چیز means 'that thing', and نیز = 'also/too' adds that the speaker will likewise accept it.
segments.s717-g113: که introduces a content clause here ('that'); بهرام is the proper name (subject); دادش is داد + enclitic ش (gave him). به marks the beneficiary/target ('for/to') and ایران is the object/place. امید is the direct object 'hope' (i.e. 'gave him hope for Iran').
segments.s717-g114: سخن گفتن is a nominalized verb phrase 'the speaking / my speaking' (گفتن as verbal noun) with من as possessor; شود is the archaic/poetic present/future copula 'become / may it be'. باد و بید are two nouns in a poetic image ('wind and willow') that form the predicate complement — 'may my speaking become (like) wind and willow.'
segments.s717-g115: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; امید خاقان is a possessive phrase 'the khagan's hope' (possessor follows the noun in poetry); بدو 'to him' (به + او contracted); تیره گشت is an adjectival predicate where تیره 'dark/bleak' precedes the past/preterite گشت 'turned/ became'.
segments.s717-g116: به بیچارگی is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in misery/with helplessness' (به used adverbially + بیچارگی). سوی خاتون 'toward / into the presence of the khatun' (سوی = toward/in the presence of). گذشت is the past tense of 'pass/go' used here as 'he went / approached'.
segments.s717-g117: همی‌جست is a habitual/continuous past 'kept searching' (همی- prefix marks ongoing action). تا here introduces the content/goal clause 'to find out'; کی + ست is the fused interrogative 'who is'; نزدیک اوی = 'near him' with اوی an older enclitic form meaning 'him'. Word order is typical poetic: verb (جست) before the subordinate clause.
segments.s717-g118: که introduces the relative clause 'who...'; روشن کند is a causative-like construction where روشن 'bright' with کند ('make/do') yields 'make bright / enlighten' (کند here a present/subjunctive or potential 'may/ might make'); جان تاریک اوی is the object 'his dark soul' (جان = soul, تاریک = dark, اوی = his).
segments.s717-g119: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / someone'; کدخدایی here denotes the role/title 'headman' (a person acting as headman). به‌دست آمدش is a poetic/idiomatic phrasing: به‌دست (into the hand/by chance) + آمدش 'came to him' — together marking that this person arrived or was brought to him.
segments.s717-g120: همان 'that same (one)' + نیز 'also/likewise' emphasize repetition; با او 'with him' marks accompaniment/presence; نشست is past 'sat (down)' and آمدش again 'came to him' — the two verbs together are a poetic doublet describing his arrival and sitting beside the addressee.
segments.s717-g105: چو = a subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خاقان is the subject 'the khagan'. شنید is the simple past verb 'heard'. این سخن is the direct object 'this speech/these words'. خیره شد is a two-word predicate: خیره (astonished/stunned) + شد (became) — a periphrastic past meaning 'became astonished'. The clause order is typical SOV with the predicate at the end.
segments.s717-g106: دو چشمش = 'his two eyes' (دو before the possessed noun; -ش is the possessive clitic 'his'). ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'from/because of'. گفتار او = 'his speech/words' (گفتار + pronoun او). تیره شد = 'became dark' (تیره 'dark/ominous' + شد 'became') — a resultive past describing a visible reaction.
segments.s717-g107: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said'). زین سان = an adverbial phrase 'in this way/so' (زین = 'this' + سان = 'such/as'), and سخن‌ها مگوی is an addressed negative imperative: سخن‌ها (plural 'words') + مگوی 'do not say' (مـ- negative prefix + گوی imperative/subjunctive stem).
segments.s717-g108: که introduces a subordinate clause of consequence/explanation ('for/that/which'), تیره کنی is a present-subjunctive/jussive clause 'you would make dark' (تیره = 'dark', کنی = 2nd-person present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/cause'), نزد ما = 'before us/in our presence' (نزد = 'in the presence of'), and آب روی is an idiomatic genitive 'the water of the face' meaning 'countenance, honour, or favour' — together: 'lest you darken our countenance.'
segments.s717-g109: نیم من here functions as an emphatic negation before the speaker 'I am not…' (an archaic negative construction), من = 'I'. بداندیش = predicate adjective 'malicious, ill‑wishing'. و = 'and'. پیمان‌شکن = 'oath-breaker, faithless' (a second predicate). The clause asserts what the speaker is not.
segments.s717-g110: که again introduces a (result/fate) clause 'that…'. پیمان‌شکن is the subject 'an oath-breaker'. خاک یابد کفن is a compact predicate: خاک 'earth/soil' + یابد 'finds/shall find' (present indicative/general-future sense here) + کفن 'a shroud' — literally 'the oath-breaker will find the earth as his shroud', i.e. he will die/bring himself to burial in the soil.
segments.s717-g111: چو = 'when' introducing the temporal clause. بشنید = past tense 'he heard' (بـ- is the classical past). خراد برزین forms a nominal phrase: خراد as an epithet 'the wise' or part of the name and برزین a personal name (together 'the wise Barzin') functioning as the subject who heard. سخن = 'speech/words' is the object.
segments.s717-g112: بدانست = 'he knew/realized'. کان is an archaic particle introducing the content of the realization (here conveying 'that/indeed/previously' in classical usage). کار او = 'his affair/plan' (کار = affair, او = his). شد کهن = 'became old/ancient' (شد = became; کهن = old) — together: 'he realized that his affair had become old/long-standing.'
segments.s717-g153: یکی تکیهٔ نامعین است placed before the noun phrase (here meaning “a certain/one”); تاج نو is the noun phrase “new crown.” بودیی is an archaic past form of بودن (third‑person past, here “there was / he had”), and the locative phrase بر سرش (“on his head”) follows the verb, giving the place where the crown was.
segments.s717-g154: به ویژه is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning “especially.” که introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason/time; بیمار شد is a past tense predicate “became ill,” and دخترش (postverbal here) is the subject “his daughter,” placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s717-g155: بدو گفت is the short classical sequence “he said to him” (بدو = به + او, dative/locative). کاین is the demonstrative/complementizer “that/this,” and دانشم is the speaker’s claim of knowledge (دانش + ـم, “my knowledge” / “I know”). نیز هست places emphasis on existence/possession: “also is” = “I have this too.”
segments.s717-g156: چو here functions as a conditional/temporal conjunction “if/when.” گویی is used in the sense “if you say (so)” (an archaic subjunctive/optative form). بسایم is a first‑person subjunctive/future‑oriented form meaning “I shall do / I will put to use / I will set about” (older formation with بـ‑). برین کار دست is the idiomatic cluster “put the hand to this work” = “undertake this task” (برین = این with Arabic/poetic spelling).
segments.s717-g157: بشد is an archaic past/inchoative verb here meaning “he went/it happened that he went” or simply “he went.” پیش + خاتون = “before the lady / to the lady.” دوان is a participial/adverbial form “running/in haste” that characterizes his motion. کدخدای follows without a conjunction to indicate the other person approached: “and to the headman” (classical poetic elision of conjunctions).
segments.s717-g158: که introduces reported speech/clause “that ….” دانا modifies پزشکی: here پزشکی functions as the person/office of medicine (a physician/medical practitioner), so دانا پزشکی = “a learned physician.” نوآمد is a compound/poetic past meaning “newly arrived.” به جای literally “to the place/post,” here “to the position” or “in his stead.”
segments.s717-g159: بدو گفت again = “he said to him.” شادان زی is an imperative formula: شادان (joyful) + زی (imperative of زیستن) = “live happily.” و links the next imperative. نوش خور is a poetic compound exhortation where نوش (drink/draught) combined with خور (imperative ’consume’) yields “drink” (i.e., take a drink).
segments.s717-g160: بیارش is the imperative ‘bring (him/it)’ (بیار + enclitic ش = “bring him/it”). مخار in this context is an imperative/command meaning “conceal / keep secret” (an archaic/poetic form); اندرین = اندر + این “in this,” and کار سر = “the matter in (your) head/heart,” i.e. keep this matter in your mind—an idiom for keeping something secret.
segments.s717-g121: سخن‌های خسرو بدو یاد کرد is built around the verbal phrase یاد کرد (یاد + کردن), an idiom meaning 'to recall/mention'. The indirect object بدو ('to him') follows the noun phrase سخن‌های خسرو ('Khosrow's words'). Classical Persian often places the indirect object before the compound verb (یاد کرد) as here.
segments.s717-g122: دل مرد بی‌تن بدان شاد کرد is a nominal subject (دلِ مردِ بی‌تن 'the heart of the man without a body') followed by the adverbial/causal phrase بدان (به + آن, 'by/with that') and the causative verb شاد کرد ('made [happy]'). Note the order: cause/means (بدان) precedes the verb; شاد کرد is a two-part idea 'to make happy'.
segments.s717-g123: بدو گفت خاتون مرا دستگیر is literal word order: بدو ('to him') + گفت ('said' — here reported speech marker) + خاتون ('the lady') as the subject of the reported clause. مرا دستگیر is shorthand for مرا دستگیر است/بود ('she is my protector/help'); م and را are split in the edition but function together as the pronominal object ('me') plus object marker. دستگیر here means 'helpful, succoring'.
segments.s717-g124: بود تا شوم بر درش بر دبیر: بود is the copular/auxiliary, تا introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause ('so that / in order that'), and شوم is the 1st‑person subjunctive 'I may become/shall be'. بر درش ('upon/at her door') uses بر + در (here در = 'door') with the possessive suffix ش ('her'). The final بر دبیر ('in/unto the scribe') indicates direction/position toward the scribe; the repeated بر is idiomatic.
segments.s717-g125: چنین گفت با چاره‌گر کدخدای places the adverb چنین ('thus/so') before the verb گفت ('said/spoke') and then specifies the companion/agent with با + noun: با چاره‌گر کدخدای ('with the resourceful headman'). چاره‌گر is an adjectival/agentive noun 'one who finds remedies/solutions'.
segments.s717-g126: کزو آرزوها نیاید بجای is a compact clause: کزو ('from whom / from which') is a relative/adverbial particle, آرزوها ('the wishes') is the subject, نیاید is a classical negative present/optative 'do/shall not come' (i.e. 'will not be realized'), and بجای ('in place' / 'to their place') idiomatically means 'to come to pass' or 'be fulfilled'.
segments.s717-g127: که بهرام چوبینه داماد اوست: که is the relative conjunction 'that/for', introducing an appositive clause. بهرام (name) + چوبینه (epithet 'Chubin' — here a byname derived from 'wood/club') form the full name; داماد اوست is a nominal predicate 'is his son‑in‑law' with the copula ست attached to the end.
segments.s717-g128: و زویست بهرام را مغز و پوست: زویست is a poetic/compressed form meaning 'is from him / belongs to him' (ز + وی + ست). The topical marker را follows بهرام (بهرام را). مغز و پوست ('brain and skin') is an idiomatic merism (inner + outer) meaning 'wholly, in mind and body.'
segments.s717-g65: بدین روز is a prepositional/time phrase 'on this day' (here split as ب + دین in the manuscript). پیوند ما is the noun + possessive 'our bond', and تازه گشت is a past intransitive construction: تازه (new/fresh) + گشت (became/turned), so literally 'our bond became new.' The verb گشت is the simple past/preterite used here to indicate change of state.
segments.s717-g66: همه کار 'all matters/affairs' is the subject; بر دیگر اندازه گشت is an idiomatic predicate 'took on another measure/shape.' بر here has the sense 'toward/to' and اندازه ('degree/extent') is the thing that changes; گشت is again the past 'became.' Word order places the prepositional/qualifying phrase after the subject in poetic register.
segments.s717-g67: ز پیروزگر 'from the Victor' (ز = 'from' in poetic diction). آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' and بر تو 'upon you' marks the beneficiary; باد is the optative particle 'may' (a conventional wish formula in classical Persian). The phrase is an optative blessing: 'May praise from the Victor be upon you.'
segments.s717-g68: سر نامداران زمین is a genitive compound 'the head/leader of the famed of the earth'—سر used here in the sense 'chief/foremost.' تو is the addressee, and باد again is the optative 'may,' forming another wish: 'May you be the foremost of the earth's renowned.'
segments.s717-g69: همی‌گفت is the iterative/imperfective 'kept saying' (common in epic/formal Persian). و links the clauses. خاقان بدو داده گوش is literal 'the khagan gave ear to him'—an idiom meaning 'the khagan listened to him'; داده is the perfect participle 'given' used in the light-verb construction with گوش.
segments.s717-g70: چنین گفت = 'he said thus' (چنین 'thus' + گفت, here functioning as the speech verb). کای is the archaic/vocative particle 'O', addressing someone: 'O man, seller of learning' (مرد دانش‌فروش). دانش‌فروش is a compound in which فروش functions as 'seller' (= one who sells learning).
segments.s717-g71: به ایران 'in Iran' (prepositional phrase). اگر نیز چون تو کسست is a conditional clause: اگر نیز 'if even', چون تو 'like you', کسست = کس است 'there is someone' (a contracted classical form). The construction expresses 'if there is anyone like you in Iran...'.
segments.s717-g72: ستاینده آسمان is 'the one who praises the heavens' or 'heaven's praise' (ستاینده = praiser/that which praises), او بسست is a terse predicate 'is enough for him' (بسست = بس است, contracted). Together: 'the praise of the heavens is sufficient for him.' The clause uses the contracted classical forms common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s717-g81: وگر نه (literally “but if not”) opens an alternative conditional/contrast: “or if not/otherwise.” ز here is the poetic preposition meaning “from/by.” هدیه تو is a genitive phrase “your gift.” روشن‌تری is a comparative adjective (“brighter/more shining”) with the -ترِی ending; it functions as the predicate describing the (implied) subject “you.”
segments.s717-g82: به introduces the beneficiary/target (“for/to”) and دانندگان جهان is a genitive construction “the knowers/learned of the world.” افسری is a noun meaning “a diadem/ornament” placed after روشن‌تری to complete the idea: “(you are) a brighter diadem for the world’s learned.” The sequence places the predicative noun phrase after the comparative adjective, typical in poetry.
segments.s717-g83: یکی here means “one/a single” (emphasizing a single spot). جای خرم is a noun phrase “a joyous/pleasant place” (خرم = joyful). بپرداختند is past tense, 3rd person plural, from پرداختن with the prefix بـ; here it means “they attended to/prepared” (not just paid money). The verb applies to the preceding noun phrase: “they prepared one joyous place.”
segments.s717-g84: ز is the poetic partitive/prepositional “from.” هر گونه‌ای is a partitive expression “of every sort/kind” (گونه‌ای = “a kind/type” with the -ای indefinite). جامه‌ها is the plural “garments/robes.” ساختند is simple past, 3rd person plural of ساختن “they made.” The whole clause means “from every sort they made garments.”
segments.s717-g85: The repeated به introduces a series of circumstantial locations/occasions: خوان (banquet), شکار (hunt), بزم (feast/assembly), می (wine). The coordinating و simply links the items. Each noun is governed by به to mean “at/in/for the banquet, the hunt, the feast, the wine.”
segments.s717-g86: به نزدیک is a locative phrase “near/in the presence of.” خاقان is the title “Khaghan” (the ruler). The final sequence بدی نیک‌پی appears here as a personal name or epithet placed after the locative (a typical poetic inversion): “Badi‑Nīk‑pī (the named person) was near the Khaghan.” Because the two words function as a proper name/epithet in context, they are treated as a name rather than their ordinary lexical senses.
segments.s717-g87: همی‌جست is an imperfective past/habitual past form meaning “he kept seeking/kept looking.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” روزیش (roozish) here means his fortune/daily provision (poetic use of روز), جایی “somewhere,” and بیافت is past-subjunctive/optative-like “to fall/befall” (i.e. “his fortune befell/fell to some place” = he found a place/position). The clause says his sustenance/fortune came upon some place.
segments.s717-g88: به مردی is a dative/goal phrase “to/for a man” (مردی = a certain man). به گفتارش literally “to/for his speech” means “toward speaking with him” or “to his address.” اندر is the preposition “in/into” used here with شتافت (past of شتافتن) meaning “he hastened/rushed.” Together the clause means “he hurried up to a man and to speak with him.”
segments.s717-g169: کجا is an adverb here (literary/poetic 'there' or 'where'); تره‌گر is the agent 'herb‑seller' and precedes the verb as often in poetry; کاسنی is a predicative noun ('called it “kāsni”') placed before the verb; خواندش is past tense 'he called it' with the enclitic object -ش. Word order shows a poetic inversion but the structure is essentially 'تره‌گر (subject) + کاسنی (predicate/name) + خواندش (verb + object clitic)'.
segments.s717-g170: تبش is تب + possessive -ش 'his fever/ardour' (here metaphorical: desire). خواست is the simple past 'wanted/wished'. کز is the old/contraction form of که meaning 'that' (introducing the subordinate clause). مغز is 'core' and is the source/part referenced; بنشاندش is the causative/past 'he set/plant(ed) it' with enclitic -ش referring back to the thing. Overall: 'his desire wanted that he should plant/set it from the core.'
segments.s717-g171: به فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' (به used instrumentally/agentive). چو functions as 'when' introducing a temporal clause; شد here means 'passed/was' (happened) and combines with هفت روز to mean 'when seven days had gone by.' The time expression follows شد in the poetic order.
segments.s717-g172: شد is the simple past 'became/was'; آن دخت 'that maiden' is the patient/subject and appears after the verb by poetic inversion. چون is the simile 'like/as'; ماه is 'moon' used as a beauty metaphor; گیتی‌فروز is a compound adjective 'world‑illuminating'. Together: 'that maiden became like a world‑illuminating moon.'
segments.s717-g173: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'she/they brought' (here third singular); دینار is the direct object 'dinars'; خاتون 'the lady' is the subject but placed after the verb (poetic inversion). ز گنج is a prepositional phrase 'from the treasure' with poetic preposition ز (= از).
segments.s717-g174: یکی is the numeral 'one' modifying بدره (a noun used poetically — 'badreh' literally 'full moon' but here an item/name). و is 'and'. تای is a classifier/word meaning 'pair' or 'a matched set'; زربفت is the material 'gold‑brocade'. In classical/poetic Persian the numeral often follows the noun, so 'تای زربفت پنج' reads as 'five pairs of gold‑brocade.' The phrase lists the objects brought.
segments.s717-g175: بدو is the prepositional/enclitic form 'to him' (بدو = به + او) indicating the indirect object. گفت is the past 'said' (here introducing direct speech or a complement clause). کاین is contractional/classical 'که + این' meaning 'that this' or 'this which'; ناسزاوار is an adjective 'shameful/unworthy' modifying چیز 'thing'. The whole segment functions to introduce what was said: 'to him he said (that) this shameful thing...'.
segments.s717-g176: بگیر is the imperative 'take!' and بخواه is the imperative 'ask/request!' (two commands joined by و). آنچ is an older form of 'آنچه' meaning 'that which / whatever'. بایدت is the predicate 'it is necessary for you' (باید + enclitic -ت 'for you'), i.e. 'you need'; نیز 'also/too' adds 'in addition'. The sequence commands the addressee to take the thing and then to demand whatever else he needs.

sentences.s717-s5: You are more learned in Iran and Nīrān, and in speech you are the more able.
sentences.s717-s6: He opened the treasure and brought forth so many gems, swords, and a golden girdle.
sentences.s717-s7: So that Khrad (wisdom) stood astonished at Barzin; he kept calling the name of God in secret.
sentences.s717-s8: When, with gifts, he set out on the road to China, at the Jeyhun he took another route.
sentences.s717-s25: No one knows this — neither by his love nor by his hatred — except the World-Creator.
sentences.s717-s26: For we all have a single origin of the earth; to the wretched body we have given death.
sentences.s717-s27: First I came from Jam to this eminent sovereign Tahmūraš, the Praiseworthy.
sentences.s717-s28: So go on likewise up to Kay Qobad — the same famed men whom we keep in memory.
sentences.s717-s29: Also fix this mark upon Esfandiyar, as upon the famed Kay-Khosrow and Rostam.
sentences.s717-s30: In the world there was but one tomb for them; therefore they tasted poison instead of opium.
sentences.s717-s31: Now the king of Iran is in his own person; all—both the joyful and the sorrowful—are, to a lesser or greater degree, his.
sentences.s717-s32: At the time of kings there was with acclaim that his father and mother were the Khagan of China.
sentences.s717-s17: First he offered praise to the Creator, the mighty knower of fate.
sentences.s717-s18: Who created the sky and the place and time; he created the capable and the incapable.
sentences.s717-s19: That same revolving wheel, without a pillar — why should it not be under his command?
sentences.s717-s20: Know that Creator who created the world, who raised the lofty heaven and spread the earth.
sentences.s717-s13: He said to him: whenever you give a command, the tongue will open a way to speak.
sentences.s717-s14: The Khaghan said to him in sweet words: the hearts of the people, though old, become young.
sentences.s717-s15: Say those words that are of use; spoken words are the marrow, and the unsaid are the skin.
sentences.s717-s16: When Kharād Barzin heard that speech, ancient hatreds came to his mind.
sentences.s717-s1: When the news came to the great king that a wolf had strode out of the thicket.
sentences.s717-s2: Bahrām-gord musters (or 'brought') an army that would take the light from the sky.
sentences.s717-s3: The king thus said to Kharad Barzin: 'Choose this task for four months.'
sentences.s717-s4: Send one to the Khaghan — a worthless, swift runner — and tell whatever you know that must be told.
sentences.s717-s9: When he came near the khaghan’s court, he paused and chose a spokesman.
sentences.s717-s10: He appointed him to say that a messenger had come from the king to this court.
sentences.s717-s11: When the khaghan heard, he made himself ready and ordered that the ways be opened.
sentences.s717-s12: The messenger came constrained, raised his voice briefly, and offered his salutations.
sentences.s717-s69: He sent someone and summoned him, then seated him on that renowned place.
sentences.s717-s70: He gave (him) dirhams and dinars; he gave him that cloak and plentiful food.
sentences.s717-s71: When you sat at the feast and summoned him, you set him among your notables.
sentences.s717-s72: A man full of thought, very learned; patient‑hearted, clever and expert.
sentences.s717-s89: He answered thus: "Keep this; I will ask for it whenever it becomes useful."
sentences.s717-s65: You are a man of letters, a skilled problem‑solver; do not, even a weighty secret, disclose it to the wind.
sentences.s717-s66: When Kharād Barzīn heard these words, he saw neither the head of the covenant nor its foundation.
sentences.s717-s67: There was a Turk, an ill old man named Qalun, whom the Turks kept humiliated.
sentences.s717-s68: He wore nothing but a fur coat; he subsisted on kashk and millet and fared poorly.
sentences.s717-s45: Bahram kept saying, 'He is ill‑natured; an evil deed coming from Ahriman is even worse.'
sentences.s717-s46: He sold the worldly‑wise men for something that is not worth a trifle to speak of.
sentences.s717-s47: To him Hormaz raised the merchant; he raised his honor above the sun.
sentences.s717-s48: No one in the world knew his name; from the world all his desires arose (i.e., all his wishes were fulfilled).
sentences.s717-s49: If he treats you very kindly, in the end he will break his covenant with you.
sentences.s717-s50: Just so — as he was defeated by the Shah of Iran — he was neither a worshipper of Khosrow nor a worshipper of God.
sentences.s717-s51: If you send him near the king, he will lift the Shah of Iran's head to the moon.
sentences.s717-s52: After that, all China and Iran will be yours; there you shall make your rightful seat — the throne (is) by desire.
sentences.s717-s21: He is powerful, wise, and possessor; he is the painter (adorner) of heaven and earth.
sentences.s717-s22: Within the vault of the sky he created the sun; he created night and day, calm, and sleep.
sentences.s717-s23: The ability/power is his; we are his servants; we are the proclaimers of all his truths.
sentences.s717-s24: To one he gives a high crown and throne; he makes another a servant and needy.
sentences.s717-s73: For that reason he conferred with the steward of the household, and he sought counsel from the Chinese lady.
sentences.s717-s74: That same one stood before the khagan by day and by night; when you went away you kept your lips sealed.
sentences.s717-s75: Thus spoke with the elder chief that, since you are an honored man and a scribe.
sentences.s717-s76: If you show skill in your medicine, or if your name becomes famed from afar.
sentences.s717-s81: He came to Khurad Barzin and said that this secret must be kept hidden.
sentences.s717-s82: Go before him; do not tell your name; act as a physician but appear a newcomer.
sentences.s717-s83: That healer came close to the lady and, woefully, saw that her heart (liver) was ill.
sentences.s717-s84: She ordered that they bring hot water; they brought that same herb from the stream.
sentences.s717-s37: Thereupon, at that moment, he set him down somewhere and seated him close beside himself.
sentences.s717-s38: At his command he presented the gifts, and one by one he counted them to his treasurer.
sentences.s717-s39: The Khaghan said to him that, unintentionally, your origins (or sources) in the world have been diminished.
sentences.s717-s40: If you will accept anything from me, say it, so that I too may accept that thing.
sentences.s717-s57: That Bahram gave him hope for Iran — may my words become wind and willow.
sentences.s717-s58: When the khagan's hope for him turned dark, he went in misery to the khatun.
sentences.s717-s59: He kept searching to find who was near him, who might brighten his dark soul.
sentences.s717-s60: A certain headman came to him; that same man likewise came and sat with him.
sentences.s717-s53: When the khagan heard these words he was stunned; his two eyes grew dark because of that speech.
sentences.s717-s54: He said to him, 'Do not speak such words, for you will darken our countenance before others.'
sentences.s717-s55: I am not malicious nor an oath-breaker; an oath-breaker will find the earth for a shroud.
sentences.s717-s56: When Khraad heard Barzin's words he realized that that affair of his had become old.
sentences.s717-s77: A certain new crown was upon his head, especially since his daughter became ill.
